serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is approximately 2 slices of wholemeal bread (about 60 g) spread with a standard portion of butter.

nutrient_source.title: Wholemeal bread with butter — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F05020132 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Wholemeal / Whole Wheat Bread — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 69 for wholemeal bread is consistent with published values in the University of Sydney GI Database for whole wheat / wholemeal bread (GI range 65–74, medium category). The butter component does not materially alter GI. GL per serving calculated as: (69 × 34.68 × 0.60) / 100 = approximately 14.4 g per 60 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Saturated fat (≈8.5 g/100 g) and sodium (285 mg/100 g) with negligible potassium (4 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this food due to its high saturated fat content (≈8.5 g/100 g), which contributes to arterial stiffness, and its borderline sodium level (285 mg/100 g) combined with extremely low potassium (4 mg/100 g), which provides no blood-pressure-lowering offset; replacing butter with an unsaturated fat spread such as avocado is recommended.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (69), moderate carbohydrate load (34.7 g/100 g), and saturated fat (≈8.5 g/100 g) impairing insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this food cautiously: the medium GI of 69 and 34.7 g carbohydrate per 100 g produce a meaningful glycaemic load, while the high saturated fat from butter (≈8.5 g/100 g) worsens insulin resistance; portion control, pairing with protein, and substituting butter with an unsaturated fat spread are advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (≈8.5 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) from butter elevating LDL cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this food because the butter contributes approximately 8.5 g saturated fat and 0.1 g trans fat per 100 g, both of which raise LDL cholesterol and increase atherosclerotic risk; substituting butter with extra-virgin olive oil or a plant-based spread is strongly recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) and saturated fat (≈8.5 g/100 g) from butter are pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this food as the butter contributes both saturated fat (≈8.5 g/100 g, exceeding the 5 g/100 g RA threshold) and trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), both of which are pro-inflammatory and can exacerbate joint inflammation; replacing butter with extra-virgin olive oil or avocado spread is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wholemeal bread with butter is a simple, widely consumed bread-based dish consisting of sliced wholemeal (whole wheat) bread spread with dairy butter. Wholemeal bread is made from flour that retains the bran and germ of the wheat grain, giving it a denser texture, nuttier flavour, and higher fibre content compared to white bread. The addition of butter adds richness, a creamy mouthfeel, and significantly increases the fat content of the dish. Per 100 g, this food provides 332 kcal, 9.85 g protein, 17.62 g fat (of which approximately 8.5 g is saturated fat and 0.1 g is trans fat from the butter), 34.68 g carbohydrate, 4.47 g dietary fibre, and 285 mg sodium. The medium glycaemic index of 69 and moderate carbohydrate content make portion control important for blood glucose management. The high saturated fat content from butter is a concern for cardiovascular disease, hypertension, and Type 2 Diabetes management, as well as for rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory fat profile. The wholemeal component contributes meaningful dietary fibre (4.47 g/100 g), iron (4.58 mg/100 g), and calcium (145 mg/100 g). This food is commonly consumed at breakfast and is popular across Western and international dietary contexts. It is vegetarian and halal but not vegan or dairy-free due to the butter content. Substituting butter with unsaturated fat spreads such as avocado or nut butter would substantially improve the nutritional profile for individuals managing cardiometabolic conditions.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020132, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). GI value of 69 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database published ranges for wholemeal bread. Saturated fat and trans fat estimates are consistent with standard butter composition data from USDA FoodData Central.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite wholemeal bread with butter entry is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond the reported fields is not available in the HPB source for this composite entry. saturated_fat_g of 8.5 g/100 g and trans_fat_g of 0.1 g/100 g are estimated from standard butter composition (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, butter salted, NDB 01001) applied proportionally to the fat content reported by HPB (17.62 g/100 g total fat). potassium_mg value of 4.0 mg/100 g is preserved from HPB source; this unusually low value is noted and reflects the composite dish composition as reported. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g × serving_size_g) / 10000 = (69 × 34.68 × 60) / 10000 ≈ 14.4 g.
